summary: I was born in Oklahoma City on May 15, 1948, and much of my life and career has been rooted here. My family moved here from Cleveland, Oklahoma, and I attended schools like Nichols Hills Elementary and Casady School in Oklahoma City. Later, I served as the Ward 2 City Councilman and Vice Mayor here before becoming a Corporation Commissioner.

We are recording this interview in the Jim Thorpe Building, which is part of the State Capitol Complex here in Oklahoma City. This building houses the Oklahoma Corporation Commission, where I currently work as a Commissioner. It's a significant place for state government operations.

I have been serving as a Commissioner at the Oklahoma Corporation Commission since January 1989, now in my fourth six-year term. This role has allowed me to engage deeply with energy and utility regulation in the state. Before that, I worked in business and local government, but this statewide office has been my primary focus for over two decades.

I was born at St. Anthony Hospital in Oklahoma City, as were all six of my brothers. This hospital has a special place in my family's history, being the birthplace of many of us. My mother was also born nearby, though in a home rather than a hospital.

My mother grew up in Enid, Oklahoma, because her father worked for the Champlin Oil Company located there. Although she was born in Oklahoma City, Enid was where she spent her childhood. This connection ties part of my family history to that city and the oil industry.

My maternal grandfather worked for the Champlin Oil Company in Enid, Oklahoma, which is why my mother grew up there. This company was part of the oil industry that influenced my family's life in Oklahoma. It connects to the broader energy themes that have been part of my career.

My father, Guy Mauldin Anthony, was born in Cleveland, Oklahoma in 1915. He attended some school there before the family and company headquarters moved to Oklahoma City. His early years in Cleveland set the stage for our family's later life in the city.

My father graduated from Classen High School in Oklahoma City after our family moved there. This school was part of his education before he went into business. It represents the family's transition from smaller towns to the city.

I attended Nichols Hills Elementary for kindergarten through second grade. It was a public school near where I lived in Oklahoma City. After that, I transferred to Casady School for the rest of my education.

From third grade through high school graduation, I attended Casady School, an Episcopalian church-affiliated school in Oklahoma City. This school shaped much of my early education and social life. It was a significant part of my upbringing before college.

I went to The Wharton School of Finance and Commerce at the University of Pennsylvania, graduating in 1970. It’s the oldest business school in the U.S., and my father also attended Wharton, so it felt natural for me to follow that path. My time there prepared me for a career in business and public service.

The Wharton School is part of the University of Pennsylvania in Philadelphia, where I completed my undergraduate studies. My education there was foundational, and it connected me to a broader network beyond Oklahoma. Philadelphia was my home during those college years.

During the Vietnam War, I was a Second Lieutenant, but my service was deferred while I pursued education and work. The Army deferred me and many classmates, allowing me to study at the London School of Economics and later work with the U.S. House of Representatives. The war was a backdrop to my early career decisions.

I earned a Master’s degree in monetary economics at the London School of Economics in London, England, after graduating from Wharton. This was around 1970-71, and it broadened my understanding of global economics. It was an important step before returning to the U.S. to work and continue my education.

I spent a year in London studying at the London School of Economics during the early 1970s. Living there gave me international exposure and a deeper understanding of economics. It was a formative experience before returning to Oklahoma and the U.S.

After working a year, I pursued another graduate degree at Yale University, earning a Master of Arts in administrative science. This was the precursor to Yale’s current school of management. It added to my education in public administration and management.

I completed a third Master’s degree at Harvard University, earning a Master of Public Administration. This further prepared me for public service and leadership roles. Harvard’s program was the final piece of my formal education before fully entering my career.

C.R. Anthony & Company was my family’s business, founded by my grandfather Charles Ross Anthony. I started working there as a child, doing chores and working in stores during summers and holidays. I became more involved full-time around 1974 and eventually served as Chairman of the Board until the company was sold in 1987.

The headquarters of C.R. Anthony & Company was located at 6th & Broadway in Oklahoma City. As a kid, I would go there early in the morning to help with tasks like sorting mail before heading to the YMCA. It was a central place in my early work experience and family business life.

In 1972, while deferred from military service, I worked as a staff economist for the U.S. House of Representatives. I was on a special energy staff for the House Interior and Insular Affairs Committee, analyzing energy demand studies. This experience was valuable, especially as the Arab oil embargo followed shortly after.
cues: House Interior and Insular Affairs Committee

summary: The Arab oil embargo occurred in October 1973, shortly after I had worked on energy demand studies for Congress. This event heightened national interest in energy issues, which I had already been studying. It was a pivotal moment that connected to my later work as a Corporation Commissioner.

John Dingell was a Representative on the State and Foreign Commerce Committee who, after the Arab oil embargo, requested that I update my energy demand study. Although I was back in Oklahoma working for my family’s company, he sent me documents to analyze. It was an interesting interaction that showed the importance of my work.

In April 1987, the shareholders of C.R. Anthony & Company sold control to Citicorp Venture Capital. I stayed on as Chairman of the Board for a couple of years after the sale. This marked a significant transition in the family business and my career path.

I was elected as the Ward 2 City Councilman in Oklahoma City in 1979 and also served as Vice Mayor during that time. Interestingly, I was the only non-incumbent to run without drawing an opponent. This local government experience was an important step before my statewide office.

Don Nickles was a prominent Oklahoma politician who has since left the scene. With his departure, I became the longest-serving statewide elected official in Oklahoma. His career provides context for the political environment in which I have served.

Clifton Scott was the state auditor who has also left public office. With both Don Nickles and Clifton Scott gone, I am currently the longest-serving statewide elected official in Oklahoma. This highlights the longevity of my public service career.

Bruce Hagen from North Dakota was an example of a long-serving utility commissioner, having served 36 years. When I started as a Corporation Commissioner, he was the longest-serving in the nation. His tenure illustrates how elected commissioners often have lengthy careers.

North Dakota is where Bruce Hagen served as a utility commissioner for 36 years. His long tenure set a precedent for longevity in such elected positions. It provides a comparison point for my own service in Oklahoma.

Charles Ross Anthony, or C.R. Anthony, was my grandfather and the founder of our family business. Born around August 10, 1884, in middle Tennessee, he came from a sharecropping family and became an orphan at age 12. He moved to Indian Territory at a young age and built a legacy that shaped my family and career.

Philadelphia is where the University of Pennsylvania and The Wharton School are located, where I completed my undergraduate studies. It was a significant city for my education and early adult life. My father also attended school there, making it a family tradition.

Indian Territory was the destination my grandfather Charles Ross Anthony chose when he left Tennessee at age 12. He traveled there with little money and no family support, seeking a fresh start. He lived in places like Holdenville and began building the life that would lead to our family business.

Holdenville was where my grandfather stayed when he first arrived in Indian Territory. He had some distant relatives there but was largely on his own. Stories from that time include his encounters with local families and his early work experiences.

My grandfather was born in middle Tennessee, in a sharecropping family. The difficult economic conditions and his becoming an orphan at age 12 led him to leave Tennessee for Indian Territory. Tennessee is the starting point of his remarkable journey westward.

Nashville is located in middle Tennessee near where my grandfather was born. It serves as a geographic reference point for his early life before he moved west. Though he was born nearby, he left Tennessee as a young boy to seek better opportunities.

My grandfather had very limited formal education and reportedly had only reached the sixth grade of the McGuffey Readers. This reflects the humble and challenging educational background he had before moving to Indian Territory. It shows how far he came from those beginnings.

While not explicitly detailed in this interview, The Trading Post is known as a publication that has featured stories about my grandfather. It is part of the historical record that preserves his legacy and the early days of our family business. It connects to the broader history of our family in Oklahoma.
